scope_note: Second-pass R4 quality review after the fix-pass landed fixes for 49 of 50 first-pass findings. Lens: test quality, assertion strength, TDD contract fidelity, named-compromise fidelity. Does NOT re-raise pass-1 findings already dispositioned in r4-triage.md. Does NOT re-raise the 4 known fix-pass surfaced findings already known to the orchestrator (Outcome::as_node, top-level vs version::Anchor API drift, RegistrationError::determinism_class accessor deferral, register_crud_with_grants wiring).

findings.claim: The 1ms setTimeout between successive createNode calls remains in the TS crud test: `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1))` is a wall-clock sleep used to advance the HLC. Pass-1 finding #10 combined this with the napi index.test.ts 32MB allocation; the triage fix (M11) only addressed the napi-side allocation — the TS-side sleep was not mentioned in the triage table and survived into the fix-pass. On a busy CI runner with coarse timer resolution or a backpressured event loop, a 1ms sleep can resolve in 0ms of clock advance, causing `second.createdAt > first.createdAt` to flake. HLC stamping is deterministic per the spec, so the sleep is either spurious (and can be removed) or load-bearing on wall-clock (and therefore flaky).
findings.evidence: Line 48 of crud.test.ts: `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1));` between line 47 `first = ... post:create ...` and line 49 `second = ... post:create ...`. Line 52 then asserts `second.createdAt > first.createdAt` — strict inequality.
findings.fix: Drop the sleep and weaken to `second.createdAt >= first.createdAt`, OR (preferred) expose a seeded-HLC testing hook on the Engine and assert deterministic stamping without wall-clock. The Rust-side `exit_2_three_creates_list_returns_them` already asserts strict increase without a sleep — the TS test should match that pattern.

findings.claim: This test still uses the dual-acceptance pattern that was explicitly closed in the partner test `write_cas_wrong_version_routes_on_conflict` (crates/benten-eval/tests/primitive_read_write.rs:75-94). Partner test was tightened at R4 triage (m3) to accept ONLY the `Ok(r) if r.edge_label == "ON_CONFLICT"` shape — `Err(EvalError::WriteConflict)` was dropped because plan §2.5 E3 pins routed-edge semantics. The evaluator_stack copy retained the `Err(EvalError::WriteConflict) => {}` arm, re-introducing the ambiguity the triage removed for the WRITE primitive. Two tests, same scenario, one tight and one loose — R5 can silently ship either shape and only one test fails.
findings.evidence: Lines 69-73: `match ev.step(&op) { Ok(r) => assert_eq!(r.edge_label, "ON_CONFLICT"), Err(EvalError::WriteConflict) => {}, Err(e) => panic!(...) }`. Triage m3 directed this pattern to be tightened; only primitive_read_write.rs was tightened.
findings.fix: Tighten to `let r = ev.step(&op).expect("WRITE CAS routes via ON_CONFLICT edge"); assert_eq!(r.edge_label, "ON_CONFLICT");`. The stack-push-on-error-edge aspect can stay as a separate follow-up assertion (`assert!(ev.stack.len() > 0)` or similar) since that's the test's named concern, not which error shape is chosen.

findings.claim: The tightened set-equality check at M2 is correct and closes the loophole — but the declared `all` array at lines 40-53 contains all 12 variants (required for filtering). The preceding test `all_twelve_primitive_variants_exist` uses an identical `all` array at lines 17-30. Two identical 12-element array literals, one after the other, is copy-paste that could drift. Not a correctness issue — a maintenance papercut that the pass-1 review didn't flag but that surfaced as the M2 tighten added the second copy.
findings.evidence: Lines 17-30 (first test) and 40-53 (second test) both declare a local `let all = [PrimitiveKind::Read, ..., PrimitiveKind::Stream]` with identical contents. A new 13th primitive would require updating two places.
findings.fix: Extract `fn all_twelve_primitives() -> [PrimitiveKind; 12]` at module scope and have both tests call it. Trivial refactor; zero behavior change.

findings.claim: The BLAKE3-128 semantic-grep improvement promised in pass-1 finding #14 was NOT applied; this test still uses the bare `posture.contains("BLAKE3") && posture.contains("128")` pattern. A SECURITY-POSTURE.md containing `BLAKE3-256` and `128 users affected` passes trivially. The triage table's M15 fix was applied to compromise_4 (WASM) — the M15 text said "For #4: parse the CI YAML... For #6: grep for a more specific phrase" but only #4 got rewritten; #6 kept the v1 pattern. Consistent with compromise_4's semantic-tightening posture would be to grep for a specific anchored phrase like `"128-bit collision resistance"` or to assert a specific section heading.
findings.evidence: Lines 254-259 identical to the pass-1 version: `assert!(posture.contains("BLAKE3") && posture.contains("128"), ...)`. Compare to compromise_4's semantic rewrite at lines 152-215 (lines 162-170 parse Cargo.toml; 173-183 check wasm-checks.yml content semantically; 187-205 scan every workflow file for forbidden strings; 209-215 assert fixture CID). Very different rigor levels for two adjacent compromise regressions.
findings.fix: Tighten to `posture.contains("128-bit collision resistance")` (anchored phrase) or parse SECURITY-POSTURE.md for a heading like `## BLAKE3 128-bit posture` and assert the section exists. Matches the rigor of compromise_4's rewrite.

findings.claim: The C3 fix added `rebuild_matches_incremental_state` tests to all three views and each asserts `rebuild() == incremental after one update`. But `rebuilt` is constructed with `CapabilityGrantsView::new()` + `.rebuild()` — the rebuild path never observed the `grant_event(ChangeKind::Created)` that the incremental path consumed. Both views end up EMPTY (the rebuild source-of-truth has no data; the incremental has one grant that the query then retrieves). So `assert_eq!(a, b)` compares two empty `Cids`, which tautologically passes whether the incremental view actually stored the grant or not. The test name claims a strong property (rebuild matches incremental); the body asserts the weakest possible satisfaction of it.
findings.evidence: view1 lines 75-82: `let mut incremental = CapabilityGrantsView::new(); incremental.update(&grant_event(...)).unwrap(); let mut rebuilt = CapabilityGrantsView::new(); rebuilt.rebuild().unwrap();`. The `rebuild()` has nothing to rebuild from (no shared event log or graph backend is passed in). Lines 87-95: the `read` on both yields `Cids(a)` and `Cids(b)`. For the canonical-CID query on view1, the incremental path populated the anchor→{cid} map so a=[cid]; but for view2's `event_name: Some("user:signed_up")` and view5's `anchor_id: Some(1)`, neither the incremental nor rebuild path populates the query key (view2 events carry label="SubscribesTo" not event_name="user:signed_up"; view5's anchor_id=1 isn't indexed to the fixture CID). So views 2 and 5's rebuild-matches assertion is `Cids([]) == Cids([])` — tautology.
findings.fix: Either (a) have `rebuild()` take a source-of-truth parameter (e.g. a slice of events) and pass the SAME events the incremental path consumed, so rebuild actually rebuilds — then the equality assertion is load-bearing; or (b) drop the rebuild-matches tests from views 2 and 5 (where the current query doesn't populate from the fixture anyway) and keep only view1 where the assertion is meaningful. Option (a) is the right fix; option (b) at least stops the claim from being misleading.

positive_notes: C1 (scaffolder regex): correctly fixed to `/\bit\s*\(/g` (single-escape). Meta-test will now count real `it(` tokens in the template.

C2 (TOCTOU): both files (compromises_regression.rs and toctou_iteration.rs) now assert `successful == 100` with identical lower and upper bounds. The ambiguity between 149 and 200 is gone. Cross-file consistency comment pins the `DEFAULT_BATCH_BOUNDARY = 100` constant.

C3 (IVM views 1/2/5): each view now has a populated-specific-CID assertion (view1 line 47-63, view2 line 41-56, view5 line 42-62) and deletion/revocation coverage. A no-op `read` implementation now fails the specific-CID tests.
M5 (IVM lag): now two-sided — `lag <= 1` AND `observed > 0` liveness check. Tautology closed.
M6 (MVCC snapshot): body rewritten to open snapshot, commit concurrently, assert pre-write state visible, drop snapshot, assert post-write visible. Name now matches body.
M9 (option_a_existence_leak): now asserts distinct ErrorCode variants, canonical catalog string, AND SECURITY-POSTURE.md documents option A. Self-comparison tautology replaced with three real checks.
M10 (float_nan_inf): file deleted; value_float.rs folded in the three-bit-pattern NaN test and the finite-extremes positive-boundary test.
M13 (durability_modes bench): `todo!()` sentinel panics added at both bench entry points — silent fall-through to default `RedbBackend::open` is now a loud failure until G2 wires DurabilityMode.

M14 (NoAuthBackend API drift): noauth.rs, noauth_proptest.rs, and production_refuses_noauth.rs all use `NoAuthBackend::new()` + `WriteContext { target_label, is_privileged, actor_hint, ..default() }`. Three-file convergence complete.

M15 (compromise_4 WASM): semantic-rewrite parses Cargo.toml for `wasmtime =` declarations, checks wasm-checks.yml contains `wasm32-unknown-unknown` and `cargo check`, and scans every workflow file for forbidden `wasm32-wasip1`. No more naive string-grep.

M16 (proptests): 5 new proptests landed (subgraph_cid_order_independent, edge_roundtrip_cid_stable, kvbackend_laws, value_json_cbor_conversion, transform_deterministic). All structured as red-phase against todo!() stubs; contract shape is good.

M24 (transform_grammar_rejections): new `assert_rejected_with(expr, token)` helper computes offset from `.find(token)` instead of hardcoded integers; legacy `assert_rejected_at` retained only for offset-0 cases. Parser-shift-tolerance preserved where it matters.
m7 (engine_builder_thinness): renamed to `thin_engine_has_no_ivm_subscribers_when_disabled`; honest about scope.
top_three_concerns: The IVM view rebuild-matches-incremental tests (views 2/5 specifically) assert equality between two empty results because `rebuild()` has no source-of-truth to rebuild from. View 1's variant works (its query populates). This is a residual C3 issue the fix-pass didn't fully close — R4b re-review after R5 should verify this test is actually load-bearing, not vacuously passing.
The BLAKE3-128 compromise regression (compromise_6) retained the pass-1 weak-grep pattern while compromise_4 got a full semantic rewrite. Inconsistent rigor for adjacent compromise tests.

A small cross-file tightening gap: primitive_read_write.rs's WRITE CAS conflict test was tightened to ON_CONFLICT-edge-only; evaluator_stack.rs's partner test kept the dual accept pattern. R5 could ship either shape and only one test fails.
notes_for_r4_consolidator: All 3 pass-1 criticals landed correctly. The C3 fix has a residual minor weakness in rebuild-matches-incremental for views 2/5 (see finding #5), but the headline vacuous-pass concern is gone.
This pass-2 review produced 0 critical, 0 major, 5 minor findings against a 49-fix delta. Net quality trajectory is strongly positive.

The 4 orchestrator-known new findings from the fix-pass (Outcome::as_node, version::Anchor API drift, determinism_class accessor, register_crud_with_grants wiring) are appropriately out of scope here and were not re-raised.
No new critical- or major-severity issues emerged.
